description: Source of truth for the tools/ directory. The Tools focus group restores everything here into tools/, then fetches the external binaries listed below. Anything that CANNOT be fetched automatically lives here as a vendored file, so it can always be restored.

externalTools.name: vgmstream-cli
externalTools.purpose: Decodes Wwise Vorbis (.wem) so the Wwise Audio browser can PLAY files in the browser. Every .wem in this game is format tag 0xFFFF (Wwise Vorbis) -- no browser plays it and ffmpeg cannot decode it. Downloads of the raw .wem work without this; only playback needs it.
externalTools.notVendored: Third-party binary with its own license and release cadence. Pinning a stale copy inside this repo would age worse than fetching the current one -- and it's ~6.5MB, which has no business in git.

externalTools.name: ffmpeg
externalTools.purpose: Re-encodes decoded audio to Ogg Vorbis (~5x smaller than WAV). OPTIONAL: without it, WAV downloads and in-browser playback still work — vgmstream produces WAV on its own. Only .ogg needs this. A distro ffmpeg built WITHOUT libvorbis cannot make Ogg, which is why the app checks the encoder list rather than merely checking that ffmpeg exists.
externalTools.notVendored: ~70MB binary with its own license. Fetched on request, never committed.

notes: The server performs this restore, NOT the Python pipeline -- because the pipeline lives IN tools/, so it cannot rebuild the directory it was just deleted from.
Directories are created by the running server process, which is exactly why this exists: files copied into the container from outside end up owned by the wrong user and become read-only to the app.

pythonPackages.purpose: Pure-Python/wheel deps the texture tools need (analyze_texture_maps.py, normal_to_s.py). Installed with `pip install --target tools/pylibs`, which needs NO root and does NOT touch the system Python -- which matters in a container where the app can't write to site-packages and `--break-system-packages` is refused. The server puts this directory on PYTHONPATH when it runs the tools.
